summary: Hormuz MOU reopens oil artery as Fed rate-hike signals drain crypto and gold
causalChain: Iran's blockade of the world's most critical oil chokepoint since February fueled a global energy-inflation spiral; the US-Iran deal to reopen it for 60 days provides short-term relief, but nine Fed officials signaling a 2026 rate hike triggered $3B in Bitcoin ETF outflows and pushed gold off its highs — a classic risk-asset retreat when cash starts paying more.

table.angle: Iran-US Hormuz MOU: 60-day reopening pauses oil supply shock
Fed holds at 3.50-3.75%; 9 of 19 officials now favor 2026 hike; 10-yr yield 4.53%
Gold slips below $4,150 as rate-hike expectations outweigh geopolitical fear
Dollar flat near 100.09 caught between tariff drag and rate-hike support
Banxico cuts GDP forecast to 1.1%; Q1 contracted 0.6%; benchmark rate at 6.75%
USMCA July 2026 review: auto-parts compliance jumped 45%→89% but tariff risk lingers
Record $40.9B FDI nearshoring; peso 17.5-18.5/USD; wages 25% below China
BoJ hikes to 1.0% (highest since 1995) but yen still near 161.5/USD — weakest since 1986
Nikkei falls 0.6% to below 72,000 as investors lock in AI/chip rally profits
PBOC nudges yuan lower slowly to protect export competitiveness under tariff pressure
ECB holds rates; Middle East war spikes eurozone inflation forecast to 3.1% in Q2
DAX +1.59%; German ZEW sentiment turns positive for first time since war began
RBA holds at 4.35%; unemployment rises to 4.5% after three hikes this year
Iron ore near $100/t as China shifts to scrap-electric furnaces; Simandou supply looms
Bitcoin ~$63K; $3B ETF outflows, ~25K BTC whale sell-off over 10 consecutive days
MiCA full enforcement July 1: 80%+ EU firms unlicensed; USDT already banned
SEC+CFTC March 2026 ruling: Bitcoin, Ethereum, XRP classified as commodities
Chemists turn methane into a hormone drug for the first time (USC · Science Advances)
Oxford engineers yeast that feeds starving bees, raising 15x more young (Nature)
Physicists confirm 86-year-old quantum prediction that could unlock dark matter detection

drafts.text: Oil prices sit near $90 a barrel after Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz — a sea lane carrying roughly 1 in 4 barrels of oil shipped globally — since February. Yesterday the US and Iran signed a deal to reopen it for 60 days. Every day it was closed, fuel costs crept higher worldwide, pushing up prices at the pump, for airlines, and for ships carrying goods everywhere. The deal eases pressure, but nobody is betting it holds.

The US government currently pays about 4.53% a year to borrow for 10 years — near a multi-decade high. The US central bank held its key rate unchanged at 3.50–3.75% for the fourth straight meeting, but 9 of its 19 officials now want at least one rate increase before year-end. Higher US borrowing costs pull global money into dollars, squeeze emerging markets, and make assets like gold and crypto less competitive.

Gold slipped below $4,150 per ounce after touching recent highs. War and instability normally push gold up — it's the classic safe-haven asset. But traders are now pricing in a US central bank rate hike, and when rates rise, earning interest on cash becomes more attractive than holding gold, which pays nothing. Geopolitical fear pushed it up; the prospect of tighter US money policy is pulling it back.

The dollar sits near flat against major currencies, caught in a tug-of-war. US tariffs dampen global trade and reduce foreign demand for dollars — a weakening force. But higher US interest rates attract foreign money into dollar assets — a strengthening force. The stalemate keeps the dollar steady, but a shift in trade policy or central bank tone could break it sharply, and a dollar swing affects every country that prices oil or debt in dollars.

Mexico's central bank slashed its 2026 growth forecast to just 1.1% — its second cut in a row — after the economy actually shrank in the first quarter. To stimulate growth, the bank has cut its main interest rate 13 times since early 2024, now at 6.75%. But inflation remains stuck above 4%, leaving policymakers caught between an economy that needs a boost and prices that are still too high to cut further.

In July 2026 — just weeks away — the US, Mexico, and Canada formally review the trade rules governing $1.3 trillion in annual commerce between the three countries. Mexico has reformed aggressively: the share of auto parts meeting origin rules jumped from 45% to 89%. But US tariff threats haven't gone away. The review will determine whether Mexico's factory-building boom continues or whether companies start looking elsewhere.

Mexico attracted a record $40.9 billion in foreign factory investment in the first nine months of 2025 as companies moved production closer to the US to dodge China tariffs. Mexican factory wages of roughly $4.90/hour — about 25% cheaper than China — are the core draw. The peso is expected to trade at 17.5–18.5 per dollar this year, but US trade policy is the single biggest wild card for the whole investment thesis.

Japan's central bank raised its main rate to 1.0% — the highest since 1995. Yet the yen still trades near 161.5 per dollar, close to its weakest level in four decades. The gap exists because US rates remain far higher, so investors still prefer earning dollars over yen. Japan's finance minister has been calling Washington to coordinate. A currency this weak makes Japanese imports — especially fuel — significantly more expensive for ordinary households.

Japan's main stock index fell 0.6% to just below 72,000, retreating from recent record highs after a surge driven by chip and AI-related companies. Investors locked in profits rather than chasing prices higher. The pullback is small relative to the rally, but it signals growing caution about whether corporate earnings can truly justify those valuations as the economy deals with a weak yen and high import costs.

China's central bank is carefully letting its currency, the yuan, weaken slightly against the dollar rather than holding it firm. A weaker yuan makes Chinese exports cheaper for foreign buyers — critical for a factory-driven economy. But move too fast and it spooks investors and triggers capital outflows. Beijing is threading the needle: slow enough to signal control, fast enough to help exporters squeezed by US tariffs.

Europe's central bank kept its main rate unchanged as the Middle East war sends energy prices higher and clouds the outlook. Inflation in the euro zone is expected to spike to 3.1% in Q2 2026. The bank is stuck: raise rates more and risk choking an economy already growing at only 0.9%; hold steady and risk letting inflation become entrenched. War-driven energy shocks are the scenario central bankers most dread.

Germany's main stock index rose 1.59% after a widely watched survey of professional economic analysts turned positive for the first time since the Middle East war began. The survey asks economists what they expect for Germany's economy over the next six months — they now see improvement ahead. Germany is Europe's largest economy and industrial backbone, so an improving outlook there tends to lift confidence across the whole region.

Australia's central bank held its interest rate at 4.35% in a unanimous decision after raising rates three times earlier in 2026. The economy is sending mixed signals: policymakers see inflation still too high to cut, but Australia lost nearly 19,000 jobs last month and unemployment rose to 4.5%. The job losses suggest rate hikes may be slowing the economy faster than intended — a sign a future cut could come sooner than expected.

Iron ore — the rock Australian miners like BHP and Rio Tinto dig up and ship to China to make steel — trades near $100 per tonne. China buys roughly 70% of all seaborne iron ore, but is gradually switching to electric furnaces that use recycled scrap instead, creating a structural headwind for Australian mining. A massive new mine opening in Guinea, West Africa, adds fresh supply. Both forces push prices — and miner profits — lower over time.

Bitcoin fell to roughly $63,000 in a sharp correction. The main driver: investors pulled about $3 billion out of Bitcoin investment funds over 10 straight trading days, while large holders sold nearly 25,000 coins in a single week. The trigger is the same force hitting gold: nine US central bank officials now favor raising interest rates, making cash-paying assets more attractive and reducing appetite for volatile, yield-free bets like crypto.

Europe's new crypto rulebook takes full effect on July 1 — 8 days away. More than 80% of the 1,200+ digital-asset companies that operated under older national licenses still haven't converted to the new EU-wide authorization. Tether's USDT — the world's most widely used digital dollar, with over $140 billion in circulation — is already banned from EU-regulated exchanges after refusing to apply for a license. Companies missing the deadline must stop serving EU customers immediately.

In March 2026, US financial regulators jointly declared that Bitcoin, Ethereum, and XRP are commodities — the same legal category as oil or wheat — not investment contracts. This ended years of legal ambiguity that had prevented many banks and funds from handling these assets freely. Crypto exchanges no longer face the threat of being forced to register as stock brokers for listing them, removing one of the biggest institutional barriers to digital-asset adoption.

Chemists at CiQUS (University of Santiago de Compostela), led by Martín Fañanás-Mastral, turned methane — the main component of natural gas and a greenhouse gas — into a hormone therapy drug for the first time. Using a custom iron catalyst, they forced an almost chemically inert gas into a usable pharmaceutical building block. If scaled, the process could convert an abundant climate pollutant into medicine. 📄 Published in Science Advances · More: https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/02/260227071916.htm

Oxford researchers engineered a yeast producing fats that bees can no longer find in modern farmland. These fats — sterols — are essential for bee larvae to survive. Colonies given the supplement raised up to 15 times more young. Large-scale monoculture farming has stripped wildflower diversity enough to leave bees chronically malnourished across farm landscapes. Lab-engineered nutrition may be key to slowing global pollinator collapse. 📄 Published in Nature · More: https://www.nature.com

Physicists at the Chinese Academy of Sciences confirmed a quantum effect predicted 86 years ago: a fast-moving particle hitting an atomic nucleus shakes loose electrons in a measurable signal. Dark matter detectors need exactly this — it lets them sense particles far lighter than any current instrument can detect. A prediction from 1939 just became a practical tool for one of physics' biggest hunts. 📄 Published in Physical Review Letters · More: https://physics.aps.org
